food_subgroup: Spiced basmati rice cooked with marinated chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic biryani concept but no specific Singapore Indian Chicken Biryani entry. Nearest parent is a spiced rice dish with meat. A new concept is recommended for this Singapore-specific variant.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite rice dishes — spiced rice with chicken (biryani style) (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Singapore Indian Chicken Biryani; A03SL (composite rice dishes) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for chicken biryani at the specificity required. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Singapore Indian Chicken Biryani weighs approximately 450 g, as referenced from verified portion data, and represents a complete main-meal serving typically including rice, chicken pieces, and accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code EI000014
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN food code EI000014 for Chicken Biryani. Trace minerals copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — basmati rice (cooked)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked basmati rice, which is the primary carbohydrate component of chicken biryani. Basmati rice has a consistently lower GI than jasmine or white long-grain rice. The high protein (9.2 g/100 g) and fat (6.5 g/100 g) content from chicken and ghee further moderate the postprandial glycaemic response of the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 × 83.25) / 100 ≈ 45.8 for a 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 425 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1913 mg per standard 450 g serving, which represents approximately 83% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 300 g per meal and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as dalcha or pickled acar to keep total sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Basmati rice GI of approximately 55 (low) is more favourable than jasmine rice, but a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 83 g of carbohydrate, producing a high glycaemic load of ~45.8.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume chicken biryani in a reduced portion of 200–300 g, pairing it with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and should monitor blood glucose after consumption given the substantial carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 2.2 g per 100 g (approximately 9.9 g per 450 g serving) from ghee and chicken skin, combined with cholesterol of 38 mg per 100 g (~171 mg per serving) and high sodium, presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request biryani prepared with minimal ghee, choose skinless chicken, limit serving size to 300 g, and avoid high-fat accompaniments such as kurma gravy to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich spice profile including turmeric (curcumin), garam masala, and whole spices provides anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; lean chicken protein supports muscle maintenance.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken biryani is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice content; however, high sodium may exacerbate fluid retention in some patients and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Indian Chicken Biryani (Nasi Briyani Ayam) is a fragrant, layered spiced rice dish of marinated chicken pieces slow-cooked with long-grain basmati rice using the traditional dum (sealed pot) method, flavoured with whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaves, saffron, ginger-garlic paste, and ghee. The chicken is first marinated in yogurt, turmeric, chili powder, and biryani masala before being layered with partially cooked basmati rice and sealed for slow steam-cooking, allowing the rice to absorb the aromatic juices of the chicken. The Singapore version, commonly found at Indian Muslim restaurants, mamak stalls, and festive catering events, is typically served with raita (yogurt condiment), dhal curry, kurma gravy, and crispy fried onions. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g plate provides approximately 734 kcal, 41 g protein, 29 g fat, and 83 g carbohydrate. Basmati rice contributes a lower glycaemic index (~55) compared to jasmine rice, making this dish a more metabolically favourable rice option, though the high glycaemic load per serving (~45.8) and sodium content (~1913 mg per plate) require portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The dish is halal and gluten-free, making it widely accessible across Singapore's multicultural population.

can a diabetic eat chicken biryani
basmati rice vs jasmine rice GI which is lower

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN (Food Code EI000014), an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes. GI value referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for basmati rice, the primary carbohydrate component. Trace minerals copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. Ontology anchored to FoodEx2 A03SL as closest defensible parent. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: Portion data: multiple GrabFood listings show 500–600 g plates; the verified default portion of 450 g from ICMR-NIN is used as the canonical serving size for nutrient calculations. Duplicate portion entries in the input (multiple 550 g and 350 g entries) have been consolidated. Glycaemic load per serving is calculated as (55 × 83.25) / 100 = 45.8 for a 450 g serving (83.25 g carbohydrate = 18.5 g/100 g × 450 g). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by ICMR-NIN for this food code and are set to -1. Water content is not reported and is set to -1. The hypertension suitability flag in the input was marked 'suitable: true' despite sodium of ~1913 mg per serving; this has been corrected to concern_level 'high' in health_context to reflect clinical reality. FoodOn URI is null as no exact concept exists; gap note provided. SNOMED CT code is null; gap note provided.
